yaml.title: Problem Set 6 - Solutions
yaml.subtitle:
Economics 100B - Intermediate Microeconomic Theory
Prof. Muehlegger
yaml.author: Prof. Muehlegger

markdown.markdown:
A perfectly competitive industry produces a good with inverse demand curve P = 200 – Q and inverse
supply curve of P = 20 + Q.

In addition, the production of the good imposes costs on a neighboring town where marginal external costs
are given by P = Q.

(a) What is the competitive equilibrium?

The competitive equilibrium is where the supply curve intersects the demand curve. Setting $200 - Q = 20 + Q$ and solving for $Q$ gives $Q = 90$. Substituting $Q = 90$ into the demand equation gives $P = 110$.

So, the competitive equilibrium is at price P = 110 and quantity Q = 90.

(b) What is the welfare maximizing level of output?

The welfare maximizing level of output is where the marginal social cost (MSC) equals demand. The MSC
is the sum of the marginal private cost (MPC) and the marginal external cost (MEC).

In this case, MPC is given by the supply curve (P = 20 + Q) and MEC is given by P = Q.

So, MSC = MPC + MEC = 20 + 2Q.

Setting this equal to the demand curve (P = 200 - Q) and solving for Q gives Q = 60. Substituting Q = 60
into the demand equation gives P = 140.

So, the welfare maximizing output is at price P = 140 and quantity Q = 60.

(c) Draw the relevant graph, clearly labeling the inverse demand curve, marginal private cost curve,
marginal social cost curve, the competitive equilibrium, the welfare maximizing outcome and the
deadweight loss in the unregulated market.
                                     MSC
                                                     MPC

                                                      D

(d) Calculate the amount of deadweight loss.

In this case, the area is given by the red triangle above.

The base is (90 – 60) = 30.

The height is given by the difference between the MSC and MPC both evaluated at Q = 90. The height is
given by (200 – 110) = 90

Thus, DWL = \frac{1}{2} * 30 * 90 = 1350.

(e) What is the welfare-maximizing Pigouvian tax?

The welfare-maximizing Pigouvian tax is equal to marginal external costs at the welfare maximizing
quantity. In this case, marginal external costs at Q=60 is given P = Q = 60.

Thus, the optimal Pigouvian tax is $60.

(f) Draw the relevant graph for part (e), clearly labeling the inverse demand curve, marginal private
cost curve, marginal social cost curve, and show how the relevant curve will shift once the Pigouvian
tax in part (e) is enacted.
                                                     MPC + tax
                                   MSC
                                                   MPC

                                                   D

(g) Now, suppose that the firm is produced by a monopolist rather than firms in a perfectly
competitive industry. How much will the monopolist choose to produce?

The monopolist will maximize profits at the quantity where MR = MPC.

The monopolist’s MR is given by MR = 200 – 2Q.

Setting this equal to MPC, we have 200 – 2Q = 20 + Q -> 180 = 3Q -> Q = 60.

(h) What would be the optimal tax to levy on the monopolist if the government wanted to maximize
welfare? Briefly explain why your answer to part (h) is different than your answer to part (e).

Interestingly, in this case, the amount the monopoly chooses to produce is exactly the same as the welfare-
maximizing quantity. Thus, no Pigouvian tax is necessary. The monopolist, by maximizing private profits,
inadvertently maximizes welfare! This highlights that the incentives for the welfare maximizing policy-
maker to reduce output when negative externalities exist align with the incentives for the monopolist to
reduce output to maximize profits. Although not always true that it works out exactly, in this case, it does
and the monopolist wants to cut output just as much as the policymaker would want to reduce output.

## Question 2: Public Goods

The city of Davis operates a public pool during the summer months and can choose how many hours to
operate the pool. Suppose that it costs $16 per hour to keep the pool open.

Assume that the public pool is free to enter and can easily accommodate all 11 residents.

    -    Suppose there are eleven residents in Davis during August.

    -    The first resident is a professor with kids who has a very high valuation pool hours in the summer.
         His / her demand for pool hours per day is given by P = 20 - H
    -    The other ten residents are students, who have lower valuations for summer pool hours.     His / her
         demand for pool hours per day is given by P = 2 – (\frac{1}{10})*H

(a) Although in this case, we are assuming that a public pool is free to enter and can easily accommodate all
11 residents, explain whether in practice a public pool is rivalrous and/or excludable.

A public pool (at least for relatively low levels of attendance) is non-rivalrous. Additional swimmers don’t
impact a person’s utility of swimming, until of course the pool is packed. But, whether a public pool is
excludable or not depends on whether people can be prevented from using the public pool. For instance,
the city pools in Davis have fences around them and you have to enter through a gate where they make you
pay for admission. This is an example of a public pool that is excludable. But, a pool in the middle of an
apartment complex may not be excludable if there’s no fence / guard / gate that can prevent people from
using it. If you have a party and 40 of your friends want to jump in the pool, and there’s nothing the
complex can do to stop them, the pool is non-excludable.

(b) If the pool is financed through donations, will the pool be open? How long will it be open? Who will
pay? And who will go to the pool?

If the pool is financed through donations, only the professor contributes as only the professor has a
valuation for pool time greater than the $16/hour. The professor contributes up until the marginal benefit is
equal to the cost of an additional hour. This happens at 4 hours of pool time. The other residents free-ride
on the professor’s payment.

(c) Draw the diagram illustrating the outcomes in part (b).

       32

(d) What is the surplus generated from the pool in part (b)?

See the red region in the left panel of (f). The area under SDC but above MC reflects the surplus enjoyed
by the 11 residents if the city provides 4 hours of pool time per day.

Surplus is equal to the area of the red trapezoid. You can calculate this using the formula for a trapezoid
(b*average height) = (36 - 16)*4 = 20 * 4 = 80 or by calculating the area of the triangle and rectangle
separately = 8*\frac{4}{2} + 16*4 = 16 + 64 = 80.

(e) Now, assume that the city of Davis finances the pool through taxes. How many hours should the pool
be open to maximize welfare?

The city calculates the social demand function by vertically summing the demand curves. The social
demand curve is given by P = 20 – H + 10*(2-(\frac{1}{10})H) = 40 – 2H. In this case, welfare is maximized
where the social demand curve crosses the marginal cost of additional pool hours.

Setting SDC equal to MC, we have 40 – 2H = 16 -> H = 12. The pool stays open 12 hours per day.

(f) Draw the diagram illustrating the outcomes in part (e).

(g) What is the surplus generated from the pool in part (e)?

See the orange region in the right panel of (f). The area under SDC but above MC reflects the surplus
enjoyed by the 11 residents if the city provides 12 hours of pool time per day.

Surplus is equal to (40 – 16)*\frac{12}{2} = 24 * 6 = 144.

(h) What is the deadweight loss in part (b)?

The deadweight loss is the difference between the surplus when the good is provided by the city and when
the good is privately provided Here, the deadweight loss in part (b) is equal to 144 – 80 = 64.

                                                     MPC

                                                      D

(e) What is the welfare-maximizing Pigouvian tax?

                                                   D
